system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 5: Harmonic Fields & Scale Modes
Pages 196-205 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

  Chapter 5                                                                                               4668




  Harmonic Fields & Scale Modes:
  In the previous chapters we have learned how to build many types of chords. Now it is time to
  learn how to organize them into families that we call “ The Harmonic Fields” or "Chord
  Families". Both names work, but there will be times when we will be using similar names, so
  we should use which name best fits the situation.
  As we know, the majority of the chords are built from the superimposition of 3rd 's, and in this
  chapter we will apply that concept over the Natural Major Scale. In other words, we will build
  chords from each note from that scale forming what we call: diatonic chords. As we have 7
  notes, we will have 7 chords as well. See below:

  • Harmonic Field built from the natural major scale triads:


                                                         www             www           www     www
      &    www          www              www
                                                                                                      o
           C           Dmi              Emi              F                G           Ami       B

  • Harmonic Field built from the natural major scale 7th chords:

                                  68                     www             www           www      www
           www          www              www                                             w        w
      &                   w                w               w               w
             w
          Cmaj7        Dmi7
                       46               Emi7           Fmaj7             G7           Ami7     Bª

  Each chord, using the same order that they appear (triad or tetrad), is represented by a
  roman numeral (upper-case numerals represent major chords, and lower-case numerals
  represent minor chords): I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm. Now that we organized the chords
  into roman numerals, we will call them “Degrees". As we have 7 chords, it means that we
  have 7 degrees:


                                                                                               www
           I           IIm             IIIm             IV               V            VIm     VIIm
                                         www            www              www           www
      &    www          www                                                w             w       w
             w            w                w              w
          Cmaj7       Dmi7              Emi7           Fmaj7             G7            Ami7    Bª
177                           2019 © Jupiter Musical Group Ltd. All Rights Reserved


The 7 Modes:                                                                                          4668




If we start the natural major scale each time from different note, we will have 7 different scales.
We call them “The 7 Modes”. Each mode will receive a specific name and has its own
harmonic characteristics. Below we have the 7 degrees indicating the mode name and the scale
notes:

I = Ionian: C, D, E, F, G, A, B

II = Dorian : D, E, F, G, A, B, C

III = Phrygian: E, F, G, A, B, C, D

IV = Lydian: F, G, A, B, C, D, E

V = Mixolydian: G, A, B, C, D, E, F

VI = Aeolian: A, B, C, D, E, F, G

VII = Locrian: B, C, D, E, F, G, A

It is important to understand that from now on we will use the modes for everything, including
harmony and melody, because they can show us all the possible tensions for each chord and
also which is the correct scale for each situation. So, you must memorize the names and the
scales notes from each one of the modes.

Types of Notes in the Modes:     68
a) Chord Notes:
                       46
   - Triads (1, 3, 5) or Tetrads (1, 3, 5, 7).

b) Extended Notes:
  - Tension notes or coloring notes (9, 11, 13).

c) Avoid Notes:
  - Scale notes that cause harmonic conflict with the chord notes. Melodically they can sound
    interesting in the middle of a melody, but never as an ending note.

d) Characteristic Notes:
  - Scale notes that give the mode it's characteristics.


                             2019 © Jupiter Musical Group Ltd. All Rights Reserved               178


  Why learn the Harmonic Field?                                                                         4668




  Almost every jazz standard, R&B, soul, funk and rock song that we know is organized using
  the rules of the harmonic field. Of course there are many songs that do not use this system. We
  will learn about them later. A song is built from many parts, and all of them have chords that we
  consider as “the main harmony of the song”.

  In order to make it easy to understand, let’s think about a neighborhood where we can find a
  bakery, a pharmacy, a guitar shop, and our home. So, we consider these places as being the
  harmonic field chords, which give us a sense of harmonic organization and direction. Without
  these “main places”, we would be totally lost in our own neighborhood and the same would
  happen in the harmonic field of our song.




  Major and Minor Harmonic Fields:
  Now that you know the importance of the harmonic field, let's split this concept in two:


  1) Natural Major Harmonic Field (also can be called Major Harmonic Field)


  2) Natural Minor Harmonic Field (also can be called Minor Harmonic Field)

                                68
  But what is the Natural Minor Harmonic Field ?


                       46
  Just like the scales, every major key has a relative key and vice-versa, which is a kind of
  complement that is the opposite of it. For example: basically, we have 2 types of songs: "happy
  sounding", these are related to the major scale, and "sad sounding", these are related to the minor
  scale. Both of them complement each other just like hot and cold, black and white, male and
  female, and so on. Summarizing, we will use the Natural Major Harmonic Field to organize the
  “happy sounding songs” and the Natural Minor Harmonic Field to organize the “sad sounding
  songs”. In the following pages, we will better understand that both harmonic fields use the same
  chords, the only difference is the way you analyze them. We have many songs that use both
  harmonic fields and sometimes in different keys.

  Note: When necessary we can use sharps (#) or flats (b) together with the degrees.
  Example: bII, bIII, #IV.....



179                         2019 © Jupiter Musical Group Ltd. All Rights Reserved
